state.title: scripts/setup.sh

state.output:
A cross-platform material you and base16 color generation tool
(pronounced: mat-uh-gen)



Installation · Wiki · Themes

Donations through Ko-Fi are welcome!

Themes

#### Templates for programs and websites - [InioX/matugen-themes](https://github.com/InioX/matugen-themes)

Description

### Features > [!TIP] > If you would like to learn more about the features and configuration, read the wiki here. - **Templating engine built with Chumsky (designed for colors)** - [Read More](https://iniox.github.io/#matugen/templates) - Custom engine focused on making color manipulation simple and efficient - Import a color once in any format and automatically access all other formats (`hex`, `rgb`, `rgba`, `hsl`, etc.) - Colors are parsed as real color objects, not strings, making filters faster and more reliable - Supports piping, nested expressions, conditionals, loops, filters, includes, arithmetic operations, escaping output. - Can be used as a standalone templating engine. You can import custom json files or define and override them in the CLI. - **Generate / Export Material You and base16 color palettes** - Generate a full Material You or base16 palette from either an image or a single color - Export the generated palette as JSON or reference palette keywords directly within templates - Easily integrate palette values into config files, themes, or style templates - **Keyword Filters** - [Read More](https://iniox.github.io/#matugen/filters) - Modify any keyword using filters such as `replace`, `to_upper`, `to_lower`, and `set_lightness` - Includes built-in color filters for adjusting hue, saturation, lightness, opacity, and more - Filters can be chained together for powerful inline transformations - **Custom Keywords / Colors** - Import any JSON file (through CLI or config) and use its contents directly inside templates - Imported colors receive full multi-format support, just like built-in palette colors - Useful for adding custom theme data, config variables, or full color schemes - **Palette Customization** - Adjust contrast, lightness and choose the scheme type (light, dark, or custom variants) - Fine-tune the generated palette to match your preference or application theme ### Other projects - [Mitsugen](https://github.com/DimitrisMilonopoulos/mitsugen) - For gnome-shell, based on the [old](https://github.com/InioX/matugen/tree/python) version of Matugen - [pywal](https://github.com/dylanaraps/pywal) - More color generation backends, default theme files. - [wpgtk](https://github.com/deviantfero/wpgtk) - Like pywal, but with a gui and more features.

Supported platforms

- Windows - Linux - MacOS - NetBSD

Installation

Cargo

Click to expand ```shell cargo install matugen ```

Arch

Click to expand #### AUR: ```shell yay -S matugen-bin ``` #### Arch extra repo ```shell sudo pacman -S matugen ```

NixOS

Click to expand

Add matugen to your flake inputs: ```nix inputs = { matugen = { url = "github:/InioX/Matugen"; # If you need a specific version: ref = "refs/tags/matugen-v0.10.0"; }; # ... }; ``` Then you can add it to your packages: ```nix let system = "x86_64-linux"; in { environment.systemPackages = with pkgs; [ # ... inputs.matugen.packages.${system}.default ]; } ``` This flake also provides a NixOS/Home Manager module, which can be imported by adding this in your configuration: ```nix {pkgs, inputs, ...}: { imports = [ inputs.matugen.nixosModules.default ]; # ... } ``` The module does NOT automatically symlink the files. For an example of using this module with Home Manager, see https://github.com/InioX/matugen/issues/28 Option details can be found by reading the [module](./module.nix). A [search.nixos.org](https://search.nixos.org/options)-like option viewer is planned.

NetBSD

Click to expand ```shell pkgin install matugen ``` or, if you prefer to build it from source ```shell cd /usr/pkgsrc/graphics/matugen make install ```

Acknowledgements

- [material-colors](https://github.com/Aiving/material-colors) - [wallpaper.rs](https://github.com/reujab/wallpaper.rs) - Changing wallpaper for Windows --- This program is free software; you can redistribute it and/or modify it under the terms of the GNU General Public License as published by the Free Software Foundation; either version 2 of the License, or (at your option) any later version.

state.title: https://api.github.com/repos/InioX/iniox.github.io/contents/templates/matugen?ref=main (application/json; charset=utf-8)

state.output:
Table of Contents

    Templates

    Basic Syntax

    Type Syntax Description
    Expression (Keyword) {{ expression }} Evaluates and outputs a value.
    Block <* ... *> Used for loops, conditionals and includes.
    Escaping \{{ ... }} Escapes template output so it isn't evaluated.

    Colors and variables

    You can use your own colors by importing JSON or using the to_color filter.

    Color formats

    Name Format Output
    hex #RRGGBB #470228
    hex_stripped RRGGBB 470228
    hex_alpha #RRGGBBAA #470228ff
    hex_alpha_stripped RRGGBBAA #470228ff
    alpha_hex #AARRGGBB #ff470228
    alpha_hex_stripped AARRGGBB ff470228
    rgb rgb(r, g, b) rgb(71, 2, 40)
    rgba rgba(r, g, b, a) rgba(71, 2, 40, 1.0)
    hsl hsl(h, s, l) hsl(332.2, 94.5%, 14.3%)
    hsla hsla(h, s, l, a) hsla(332.2, 94.5%, 14.3%, 1.0)
    red red (0 - 255) 71
    green green (0 - 255) 2
    blue blue (0 - 255) 34
    alpha alpha (0.0 - 1.0) 0.7
    hue hue (0.0 - 360.0) 332.2
    saturation saturation (0.0 - 100.0) 94.5%
    lightness lightness (0.0 - 100.0) 14.3%

    Material Design 3

    Material Design 3 (MD3) colors focus on personalized, accessible, and dynamic schemes, using 5 key roles—Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, Neutral, and Neutral Variant.

    Usage:

    {{ colors.<name>.<mode>.<format>}}

    Example:

    {{ colors.primary.default.hex }}
    Name Description
    primary Main brand color
    on_primary Text/Icons on top of primary
    primary_container Subtle background for primary elements
    on_primary_container Text on top of primary container
    inverse_primary Primary color for opposite theme
    secondary Less prominent accent color
    on_secondary Text/Icons on top of secondary
    secondary_container Subtle background for secondary elements
    on_secondary_container Text on top of secondary container
    tertiary Contrasting accent color for balance
    on_tertiary Text/Icons on top of tertiary
    tertiary_container Subtle background for tertiary elements
    on_tertiary_container Text on top of tertiary container
    error Color used for errors/warnings
    on_error Text/Icons on top of error color
    surface Main background for components (cards, sheets)
    on_surface Primary text color on surfaces
    surface_variant Secondary background for components
    on_surface_variant Secondary text color on surfaces
    outline Color for borders and dividers
    outline_variant Subtle divider/border color
    background The absolute background of the app/screen
    on_background Text on the main background
    shadow Color used for elevation shadows
    scrim Overlay color for modal backdrops

    Base16

    Base16 is a framework for creating consistent, syntax-highlighting color themes across various applications (terminal, editor, shell) using a standardized palette of 16 colors. It consists of 8 shades (for background/foreground) and 8 accent colors, ensuring high contrast, readability, and a unified look across different software.

    Usage:

    {{ base16.<name>.<mode>.<format>}}

    Example:

    {{ base16.base00.default.hex }}
    Name Description
    base00 Default Background
    base01 Lighter Background (Status bars)
    base02 Selection Background
    base03 Comments, Invisibles
    base04 Dark Foreground (Used for status bars)
    base05 Default Foreground, Caret, Delimiters
    base06 Light Foreground
    base07 Light Background
    base08 Variables, XML Tags, Red
    base09 Integers, Boolean, Constants, Orange
    base0a Classes, Strings, Functions, Yellow
    base0b Strings, Inherited Class, Green
    base0c Support, Regex, Escape Characters, Cyan
    base0d Functions, Methods, Attribute IDs, Blue
    base0e Keywords, Storage, Selector, Magenta
    base0f Deprecated, Opening/Closing Embedded Tags, Brown

    Variables

    Name Description
    is_dark_mode Is true if dark mode is enabled.
    mode Dark or Light depending on the mode.

    Expressions

    Use dot notation to access nested values inside context objects.

    {{ colors.primary.default.hex }}
{{ palettes.error._99.hex }}
{{ mode }}

    Example:

    {{ custom.red_color.hex }} -> #470228
{{ custom.red_color.rgb }} -> rgb(255, 0, 0)

    In JSON:

    {
  "custom": {
    "red_color": {
      "color": "#470228"
    }
  }
}

    Includes

    Includes another template file, the name should be the template name you put in the config.

    If you just want to use the included template inside of another one and don't want to write it to a file, you can just comment out the output_path

    If you set a custom scheme type for the main template, the included template will follow it.

    [templates.includeme]
input_path = "./include.txt"
# The output path is optional if you just want to import the template
# output_path = "./a/include.txt"

    Example:

    <* include "includeme" *>

    Conditionals

    Conditionals allow branching logic inside templates.

    Negation with the not keyword is supported for any version above v3.1.0

    <* if {{ condition }} *>
  ...content if true...
<* else *>
  ...content if false...
<* endif *>

    If statement

    <* if {{ is_dark_mode }} *>
  Dark Mode
<* else *>
  Light Mode
<* endif *>

    If statement using negation

    <* if not {{ condition }} *>
  ...content if false...
<* else *>
  ...content if true...
<* endif *>

    Loops

    Range Loop

    Iterates over a numeric range (inclusive).

    <* for i in -10..10 *>
  {{ i }}
<* endfor *>

    Key-Value Loop

    Iterates through maps or objects, exposing both key and value bindings.

    Listing all the MD3 colors

    <* for name, value in colors *>
  {{ name }}: {{ value.default.hex }};
<* endfor *>

    Filters

    Filters transform or modify values, they are chained using the pipe symbol (|). They can support Expressions as arguments.

    Example:

    {{ colors.primary.default.hex | lighten: 20.0 | invert }}

<* for i in -10..10 *>
    actual: {{ i }} multiplied: {{ {{ i }} * 10 }} {{ colors.red.default.hex | lighten: {{i}} * 10 }}
<* endfor *>

    Arithmetic and Nested Expressions

    Expressions can be nested or contain arithmetic operations.

    {{ {{ i }} * 10 }}
{{ colors.red.default.hex | lighten: {{ i }} * 10 }}

    Escaping Output

    To render raw {{ ... }} text without evaluating it:

    \{{ colors.primary.default.hex }}

    This will output literally {{ colors.primary.default.hex }} in the result.
state.title: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/InioX/iniox.github.io/main/templates/matugen/templates.html (text/plain; charset=utf-8)

state.output:
Table of Contents

    Configuration

    Config file locations

    You can use a custom configuration path by using the -c argument

    Platform Location
    Windows C:\Users\user\AppData\Roaming\InioX\matugen\config\config.toml
    Linux/Unix /home/user/.config/matugen/config.toml
    MacOS /Users/user/Library/Application Support/com.InioX.matugen/config.toml

    Example Configuration

    The example config.toml file has comments for every option in the config.

    Basic Syntax

    Adding templates

    # config_directory/config.toml

[templates.test] # First way of adding template
input_path = '~/.config/example/template.css'
output_path = '~/.config/example'

[templates] # Another way
test2 = { input_path = '~/.config/example/template2.css', output_path = '~/.config/example2' }

    Template hooks

    • pre_hook: Executes a command inside the string BEFORE the template is generated
    • post_hook: Executes a command inside the string AFTER the template is generated
    [templates.test]
# input_path = "some/path"
# output_path = "some/path"
pre_hook = 'echo "hello world"'
post_hook = "cat /some/file.txt"
state.title: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/InioX/iniox.github.io/main/templates/matugen/configuration.html (text/plain; charset=utf-8)

state.output:
Table of Contents

    Usage

    Basic CLI commands

    Using the help command to list all other arguments

    matugen -h

    JSON output flag

    Not to be confused with --import-json flag, this one outputs the generated JSON.

    matugen <other-arguments> --json <FORMAT>

    Example:

    matugen color hex "#ffbf9b" --json rgb

    Changing the mode to dark or light

    If no mode is provided, the default is dark.

    matugen <other-arguments> -m light
     
    matugen <other-arguments> -m dark

    Generate from a color

    Use matugen -h to see all the available formats you can use.

    # Dark mode
matugen color hsl <hsl color> -m "dark"
# Light mode
matugen color hex <hex color> -m "light"

    Generate from an image

    matugen image /path/to/wallpaper/

    Importing JSON

    You can also automatically import JSON files from the config. See the [config.import_json_files] option.

    Check the configuration file here

    Order of importing JSON

    The order is from first to last imported, the later ones will overwrite data

    1. --import-json
    2. --import-json-string
    3. [config.import_json_files]

    Import JSON from file

    matugen <other-arguments> --import-json <path>

    Example:

    matugen color hex "#ffbf9b" --import-json ./example/custom.json

    Import JSON from string

    matugen <other-arguments> --import-json-string <json string>

    Example:

    matugen color hex "#ffbf9b" --import-json-string '{ "text3": "Hello from args!"}'

[config]
# If set to enabled, it will check for updates when you run the matugen command, notifying you that an update is avaiable.
# WARNING: Matugen needs to be compiled with the `update-informer` feature for this to work.
version_check = false

# The color which should be used as the source_color if no good color was found from an image.
# Can be defined in any css format.
# The cli flag will override this if used.
fallback_color = "#ffbf9b"

# Which color to choose if multiple good colors were found from an image.
# choices: [darkness, lightness, saturation, less-saturation, value, closest-to-fallback]
# The cli flag will override this if used.
prefer = "closest-to-fallback"

# Enables caching which will cache the colorscheme, custom colors, etc. into a cache folder located in
# Windows: C:\Users\user\AppData\Roaming\InioX\matugen\cache\images\<file>
# Linux: $XDG_CACHE_HOME/matugen/<file> or $HOME/.cache/matugen/images/<file>
# MacOS: $HOME/Library/Caches/matugen/images/<file>
# The file follows this naming: <image_name>.<image_extension>.<image_hash>.json
# For example: testimage.png.0cfd2d624b3d09bf6246b7be9ca79e207e7aa1189d961d44949628b99811bb9d.json
# You can modify the cache and it will then be loaded when using the same image.
# The colors are defined using the #RRGGBBAA format.
caching = false

# Changing the syntax
# Expr is for keywords like {{ <keyword> }}
expr_prefix = "{{"
expr_postfix = "}}"

# Block is for stuff like for loops etc.
block_prefix = "<*"
block_postfix = "*>"

# Imports json files just like `--import-json` does.
# Relative paths will be resolved from the path `config.toml` is in.
import_json_files = ["./custom3.json"]

# Defaults to the contrast from CLI if unspecified.
# Value from -1 to 1. 
# -1 represents minimum contrast, 0 represents standard (i.e. the design as spec'd),
# and 1 represents maximum contrast.
contrast = 1.0

[config.wallpaper]
# Whether to set the wallpaper or not
set = true

# The base command to run for applying the wallpaper.
# Keywords like {{ image }} or anything that works inside of hooks works here.
# You could also set a static color if you use the color keywords and the wallpaper setter supports it.
command = "awww img --transition-type center {{ image }}"

# For example, killing the wallpaper daemon. Usage is like a normal hook.
# pre_hook = ""

[templates.includeme]
input_path = "./include.txt"
# The output path is optional if you just want to import the template anyways.
# output_path = "./a/include.txt"

[templates.name1]
# Relative paths will be resolved from the path `config.toml` is in.
input_path = "./colors.whatever-extension"
output_path = "./a/colors-generated.whatever-extension"

# Defaults to a value of 0 if unspecified, determines the order that each template is processed.
# You can use index = n to force a particular template hook to run after everything else, and index = -n for before everything else
index = 1

# Defaults to the type from CLI if unspecified.
# One of `SchemeContent`, `SchemeExpressive`, `SchemeFidelity`, `SchemeFruitSalad`, `SchemeMonochrome`, `SchemeNeutral`, `SchemeRainbow`, `SchemeTonalSpot`, `SchemeVibrant`
type = "SchemeExpressive"

# For testing of the `--continue-on-error` flag
# [templates.ishoulderror]
# Relative paths will be resolved from the path `config.toml` is in.
# input_path = "./error-testing.txt"
# output_path = "./a/error-testing.txt"

# This will use a different input path depending on what mode you use.
# input_path_modes = { dark = "./colors.whatever-extension", light = "./colors.whatever-extension" }

# This will compare all of the colors inside the array with the color you set as `compare_to`, and returns the closest color to it.
# You can then use `{{closest_color}}` inside hooks.
colors_to_compare = [
    { name = "black", color = "#000000" },
    { name = "red", color = "#ff0000" },
    { name = "maroon", color = "#800000" },
    { name = "yellow", color = "#ffff00" },
    { name = "olive", color = "#808000" },
    { name = "lime", color = "#00ff00" },
    { name = "green", color = "#008000" },
    { name = "aqua", color = "#00ffff" },
    { name = "teal", color = "#008080" },
    { name = "blue", color = "#0000ff" },
    { name = "navy", color = "#000080" },
    { name = "fuchsia", color = "#ff00ff" },
    { name = "purple", color = "#800080" },
]
compare_to = "{{colors.primary.default.hex}}"

# Runs before the template is exported. You can use keywords here.
# If you want the command to be async, add an & symbol to the end of the command on unix.
pre_hook = 'echo "source color {{colors.source_color.default.hex}}, source image {{image}}, closest color {{closest_color}}"'
# Runs after the template is exported. You can use keywords here.
# If you want the command to be async, add an & symbol to the end of the command on unix.
post_hook = 'echo "after gen"'

# For overriding the prefix for a specific template only
expr_prefix = "{{"
expr_postfix = "}}"

# Only hex values
# https://m3.material.io/styles/color/advanced/adjust-existing-colors#1cc12e43-237b-45b9-8fe0-9a3549c1f61e
# If you want to use the original color without any harmonization, add `_source` after the name like: `{{ color_source }}`

15:     if not command -q pinchtab

17:         echo "  curl -fsSL https://pinchtab.com/install.sh | bash"
18:         return 1

20: 
21:     set -l cmd $argv[1]
22:     if test -z "$cmd"
23:         set cmd status

# OpenTelemetry LGTM stack management wrapper
# Runs grafana/otel-lgtm via Colima + Docker for local observability
#
# Usage:
#   otel start    - Start OTEL LGTM stack
#   otel stop     - Stop stack
#   otel status   - Show status
#   otel open     - Open Grafana in browser
#   otel doctor   - Verify OTEL env + container health
#   otel logs     - Tail container logs
#   otel restart  - Restart container
#   otel update   - Pull latest image
#   otel uninstall - Remove everything

function otel --description "Manage OpenTelemetry LGTM observability stack (Colima + Docker)"
    set -l dotfiles_root ~/dotfiles
    set -l otel_script "$dotfiles_root/scripts/otel/setup-otel.sh"

    if not test -f "$otel_script"
        echo "OTEL script not found at $otel_script"

parts.text:
**Considering function details**

I’m working on a function called `matugen-generate` that generates colors from images or color values. I need to ensure it doesn’t use Unicode, just plain ASCII. The function should check for a command, verify if a config file exists, and manage configurations based on whether it’s stowed or sourced. I’m considering how to handle different input types, set appropriate flags, and manage command options like dark or light modes and scheme names. I'll also implement regex for hex detection.

**Clarifying output for image**

I'm working on the `matugen image` command using the given source and mode. I need to confirm if the output path can use a tilde for user directories, as in `/.config/matugen/colors.json`. I wonder if I should expand the tilde or use an absolute path since the configuration is stored in `/.config/matugen/config.toml`. If testing from the repo and using custom config, then the output path can indeed be set relative to the user’s home directory, but I need to ensure Matugen handles the `` correctly in TOML files.

**Considering output paths**

I'm thinking about the output path for my configuration. A relative path works because the template is in the dotfiles. However, trying to direct output to `/.config/matugen/colors.json` may fail if the parent directory isn't there. If the directory isn’t stowed, the function might create output in HOME, which could lead to issues. It seems like I should use a relative output path for better organization and consider making the output path absolute to ensure it writes correctly regardless of the stowing situation.

**Evaluating symlink configurations**

I'm thinking about whether `/.config/matugen` is a symlink to the dotfiles and how stow is handling that. If I'm using stow from the dotfiles, it might symlink the whole directory into `/.config/matugen`. Using `mkdir -p` on a symlink should be okay. If not stowed, though, it might end up creating a local directory without proper config, which isn’t ideal. I should ensure the input path remains relative to the config source for reliability. I'll also look into setting up the right template and roles for Matugen while keeping the parameters clear.

**Considering lazy-loading for colorschemes**

I’m thinking about adding a lazy specification after mentioning Tokyo since it's the default. It seems that if lazy is set to true, then lazy.nvim might only load colorscheme plugins when I use the command `:colorscheme matugen`. It’s interesting to consider how LazyVim handles this, potentially loading colorschemes based on matching events. I wonder if the lazy-loading feature can effectively manage colorscheme plugins when specific commands are invoked.

**Evaluating plugin settings**

I’m spotting some potential issues with using `vim.fn.expand`. It seems like I could just use the string path to the colors file, but I'm thinking the plugin docs might expect the tilde to expand. Since the spec loaded in the Neovim context, I could need to update lazy-lock. Without installation, if we don’t run the sync command, the lock might remain unchanged. I think we need to validate the plugin situation, especially if the user isn’t running a network.

**Considering modifications to lazy-lock**

I’m thinking that modifying lazy-lock is heavier than I’d like, even if it offers an easy revert option. It might be wise to include validations, but perhaps they should be optional. This way, the user can decide based on their situation. I want to make sure any changes made feel practical without complicating things too much. Let's find a balance that allows for flexibility while still ensuring everything works smoothly for the user.

*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: .config/matugen/config.toml
+[config]
+version_check = false
+fallback_color = "#7aa2f7"
+prefer = "closest-to-fallback"
+caching = true
+contrast = 0.0
+
+[config.wallpaper]
+set = false
+
+[templates.matugen_nvim]
+input_path = "./templates/matugen.nvim.json"
+output_path = "~/.config/matugen/colors.json"
+type = "SchemeTonalSpot"

+function matugen-generate --description "Generate matugen.nvim colors from an image or source color"
+    if not command -q matugen
+        echo "matugen is not installed. Install Rust/Cargo, then run: cargo install matugen" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    set -l mode dark
+    set -l scheme SchemeTonalSpot
+    set -l source ""
+    set -l force_color false
+
+    set -l i 1
+    while test $i -le (count $argv)
+        set -l arg $argv[$i]
+
+        switch $arg
+            case -h --help help
+                _matugen_generate_help
+                return 0
+            case -m --mode
+                set i (math $i + 1)
+                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
+                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set mode $argv[$i]
+            case --scheme
+                set i (math $i + 1)
+                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
+                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set scheme $argv[$i]
+            case --color
+                set i (math $i + 1)
+                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
+                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set source $argv[$i]
+                set force_color true
+            case '*'
+                if test -n "$source"
+                    echo "Unexpected extra argument: $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set source $arg
+        end
+
+        set i (math $i + 1)
+    end
+
+    if not contains -- $mode dark light
+        echo "Mode must be 'dark' or 'light'" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    if test -z "$source"
+        _matugen_generate_help >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    set -l config "$HOME/.config/matugen/config.toml"
+    if not test -f "$config"
+        set config "$HOME/dotfiles/.config/matugen/config.toml"
+    end
+
+    if not test -f "$config"
+        echo "matugen config not found at ~/.config/matugen/config.toml or ~/dotfiles/.config/matugen/config.toml" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    mkdir -p "$HOME/.config/matugen"
+
+    if test "$force_color" = true; or string match -qr '^#?[0-9A-Fa-f]{6}([0-9A-Fa-f]{2})?$' -- "$source"
+        if not string match -q '#*' -- "$source"
+            set source "#$source"
+        end
+        matugen color hex "$source" -m "$mode" --type "$scheme" -c "$config"
+    else
+        if not test -f "$source"
+            echo "Image file not found: $source" >&2
+            return 1
+        end
+        matugen image "$source" -m "$mode" --type "$scheme" -c "$config"
+    end
+
+    if test $status -eq 0
+        echo "Generated: $HOME/.config/matugen/colors.json"
+        echo "Use in Neovim with: :colorscheme matugen"
+    end
+end
+

+    echo "matugen-generate - generate colors.json for matugen.nvim"
+    echo ""
+    echo "Usage:"
+    echo "  matugen-generate <image-path>"
+    echo "  matugen-generate --color '#7aa2f7'"
+    echo "  matugen-generate --mode light <image-path>"
+    echo "  matugen-generate --scheme SchemeExpressive --color '#7aa2f7'"
+    echo ""
+    echo "Output:"
+    echo "  ~/.config/matugen/colors.json"
+end

parts.text:
**Validating function syntax**

I need to validate the syntax, but I think there might be an issue. The Fish function uses `if test $status -eq 0` after matugen, but I'm realizing `$status` changes after the command execution. If the command fails, does the function return correctly since Fish doesn't set `-e`? 

So, I’m thinking I need to ensure the return status matches expected values. Also, I should check if `matugen color hex` accepts `--type` or if it could be shortened to `-t`. I need to confirm this in the CLI documentation.

**Testing matugen function**

I need to test if matugen is absent, which is good. I also require a potential template for the path or output. I wonder if the Fish parser will handle `#?[0...`. Regarding quotes, single quotes seem okay. The `string match -q '#*' -- "$source"` pattern is a glob pattern that matches the `#` prefix, meaning Fish often interprets `#*` as a comment unless it's quoted.

I should confirm `source` as a variable name since Fish uses it as a command. Next, I'll consider running Fish with `-n` and checking the setup syntax in the brewfile, possibly using `shfmt`. After sourcing, I can test `matugen-generate --help`, since matugen is missing. Also, running `fish -n` on the file seems to be a good idea, followed by some tests like `git diff`. But first, I need to patch the function.

parts.text:
**Fixing command logic**

It looks like the help command for a feature is getting triggered incorrectly instead of displaying the appropriate help message. I need to address this bug by ensuring the help function is parsed before checking for other commands. It would make sense to check for help arguments first, and then move the command check afterward. I should also make sure that if no arguments are provided, it returns the usage message instead of a command missing error. This might help improve clarity in the output!

**Reorganizing command checks**

I need to check if Matugen is installed before showing help or usage information to ensure clarity. Moving the command check below the parsing step seems effective. It's important to show the usage message if Matugen isn't found, especially if the source is missing. To improve the logic, I should remove the initial command check and place it after validating the source and configuration. This way, if there’s no source, it can directly show help. That sounds like a solid plan!

@@
 function matugen-generate --description "Generate matugen.nvim colors from an image or source color"
-    if not command -q matugen
-        echo "matugen is not installed. Install Rust/Cargo, then run: cargo install matugen" >&2
-        return 1
-    end
-
     set -l mode dark
     set -l scheme SchemeTonalSpot
     set -l source ""
@@
         _matugen_generate_help >&2
         return 1
     end
+
+    if not command -q matugen
+        echo "matugen is not installed. Install Rust/Cargo, then run: cargo install matugen" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
 
     set -l config "$HOME/.config/matugen/config.toml"
*** End Patch

state.title: Runs setup dry-run validation

parts.text:
**Evaluating plugin installation issues**

I just tried a dry-run for the tmux plugin with the restart watcher, and it seems to have failed. The bash tool output indicated conflicts, possibly due to a non-zero exit status. It didn't show a clear error, so I need to investigate further. Importantly, the dry-run validation didn't pass fully due to unrelated stow conflicts. Also, it created a plugin clone outside the expected directory, which may have impacted the lazy-lock. I should check the Neovim git status for any unexpected changes.

**Checking Lazy log and git status**

I might need to read the Lazy log to see what’s going on. It could provide some useful insights. Additionally, I should check the git status to ensure everything is in order. I'm curious if there are any changes that I missed or issues that need attention. Keeping track of these details might help clarify any problems I’m facing with the plugins or configurations. Let’s dig in and see what the logs reveal!

Validation caught two useful details: the Neovim spec load auto-cloned `matugen.nvim` into the local Lazy runtime, and the setup dry-run reached existing unrelated stow conflicts. I’m checking the resulting repo diffs now and won’t touch those unrelated conflicts.

parts.text:
**Considering configuration paths**

I’m thinking about whether "make these changes" is more fitting than "install it." I need to handle the actual `matugen` config location for macOS, which the docs say is at /Library/Application Support/com.InioX.matugen/config.toml, instead of /.config/matugen/config.toml. Since our Fish helper uses `-c`, I'm focusing only on the setup install without default config recognition. However, I might encounter an issue because dotfiles stow will create a symlink at /.config/matugen.

parts.text:
**Validating data types in CLI**

I want to ensure I include concise validation results rather than raw outputs. It seems the CLI has a global default value of `scheme-tonal-spot` for the `--type/-t` option. If `SchemeTypes` is a value enum likely in kebab-case, then my fish helper using `--type "$scheme"` expects a PascalCase default called `SchemeTonalSpot`, which might not be correct. I need to explore this further after I've compressed the data. The `arguments.rs` file indicates a default value but there’s a possible mismatch here.

content.summary:
(b1)

After the prior read-only matugen evaluation, user said: "Lets try implementing it but we won't commit anything, that way we can easily revert". Assistant initially replied that plan mode was active and gave an implementation plan: add `.config/matugen/config.toml`, `.config/matugen/templates/matugen.nvim.json`, `.config/fish/functions/matugen-generate.fish`, update `scripts/setup.sh` cargo installs, modify `~/neovim/lua/plugins/colorschemes.lua`, validate, and explicitly do no commits/pushes. User then said: "Make these changes".

Implementation performed so far, with no commits:
- Dotfiles worktree was initially clean and no `.config/matugen/**` existed.
- Created directory `/Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.config/matugen/templates`.

metadata.preview:
function matugen-generate --description "Generate matugen.nvim colors from an image or source color"
    set -l mode dark
    set -l scheme SchemeTonalSpot
    set -l source ""
    set -l force_color false

    set -l i 1
    while test $i -le (count $argv)
        set -l arg $argv[$i]

        switch $arg
            case -h --help help
                _matugen_generate_help
                return 0
            case -m --mode
                set i (math $i + 1)
                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
                    return 1
                end

**Patching CLI scheme**

I need to adjust the default scheme line to `scheme-tonal-spot` and list the CLI values for better clarity. Validating the scheme using fish might help us catch errors earlier, so I’ll set up a valid_schemes array. If new values are added, I'll ensure the validation doesn't block them, aiming for minimal fuss. I should mention that CLI values should use kebab-case without extraneous comments. Finally, I need robust fish support for `--scheme scheme-expressive` and ensure everything outputs correctly in ASCII.
The source confirms Clap `ValueEnum` plus a default `scheme-tonal-spot`, so the CLI wrapper should use kebab-case. I’m patching the helper defaults/help and leaving the TOML template type unchanged.

I think we should confirm whether everything is installed correctly, but maybe asking to install isn't necessary unless we have to. I should mention the changes made, including running `git diff --stat` in the dotfiles and Neovim. It might also be helpful to check the current status, even if untracked files won't show up in the diff. I need to include information on the lazy runtime side effect and how to revert the plugin manually for the user’s convenience.

**Clarifying installation instructions**

I shouldn't advise the user to "save/copy" any files; instead, I might mention reverting git changes and the option to remove the lazy clone. I need to be careful about suggesting potentially destructive commands, so it's wise to avoid that unless necessary. It could also be beneficial to clarify that I didn't install matugen or commit any changes. Being upfront with the user about these points helps ensure they're well-informed.

**Inspecting .gitignore**

I need to check the .gitignore file. It’s important to understand its contents since it determines which files are ignored in version control. I'll read through it to see what specifics are listed and ensure I'm not missing anything important that should be tracked. Keeping my project organized requires attention to detail, and I want to get this right for smooth collaboration in the future! Alright, time to dive in and review it!

One implementation detail is worth checking before I wrap: generated `~/.config/matugen/colors.json` will live inside the stowed dotfiles directory, so it should be ignored to avoid accidental commits.

parts.text:
**Considering lazy.nvim and validation processes**

I’m thinking that lazy.nvim can indeed lazy-load colorscheme plugins if they have `lazy=true`, especially when using `:colorscheme` command. Many themes already have this set in colorschemes.lua, so I need to confirm it's working. I should mention ignoring the output from "matugen config" and include validation steps. It's good to note that the setup dry-run encountered some unrelated conflicts and that I won’t run actual generation since matugen isn’t installed yet. Once everything is set, I’ll suggest running `matugen-generate --color '#7aa2f7'`, followed by `:colorscheme matugen`.

**Finalizing installation instructions**

I’m considering that if matugen isn’t installed, I should rely on either the provided setup or cargo. It might be helpful to say something like, “If you want me to test the generation, I need approval to run `cargo install matugen`.” Also, I think the final output should avoid heavy formatting and instead use bullet points for clarity. A simple title like "Implemented" works well too. This should make it all more straightforward for the user!

Implemented the matugen experiment without committing anything.

Changed:
- Dotfiles:
  - `.config/matugen/config.toml`
  - `.config/matugen/templates/matugen.nvim.json`
  - `.config/fish/functions/matugen-generate.fish`
  - `scripts/setup.sh`
  - `homebrew/Brewfile`
  - `.gitignore`
- Neovim repo:
  - `~/neovim/lua/plugins/colorschemes.lua`
  - `~/neovim/lazy-lock.json`

Notes:
- Tokyo Night remains the default Neovim theme.
- `matugen.nvim` is added as an optional lazy colorscheme.
- `matugen-generate` writes `~/.config/matugen/colors.json`, and that generated file is now ignored by git.
- I fixed the helper to use matugen CLI scheme names like `scheme-tonal-spot`, while keeping the TOML template type as `SchemeTonalSpot`.
- I did not install `matugen`, commit, or push.

Validation:
- `scripts/test-filter.sh fish` passed: 52/52
- `scripts/test-filter.sh setup-syntax` passed: 4/4
- `scripts/test-filter.sh brewfile` passed: 2/2
- Neovim plugin spec check passed, but Lazy auto-cloned `matugen.nvim` and updated `lazy-lock.json`
- `scripts/setup.sh --dry-run --profile standard` reached stow and showed `.config/matugen` would link, then stopped on unrelated pre-existing stow conflicts for Gemini, Ruff cache, and Claude Desktop config.

To actually try it, matugen still needs to be installed. I can do that next if you want, but it would change machine state beyond git-revertable file changes.
Install it then so we can test it

**Installing and Testing Matugen**

I need to help the user with installing Matugen and testing it. First, I'll confirm if Cargo is available for installation using bash commands. The user has approved installing it, so that simplifies things! There may be multiple steps: checking Cargo, installing, generating tests, and validating the installation. However, there’s some confusion over task tracking tools since I'm instructed not to use TodoWrite. I’ll stick to the right tools and provide a progress update before moving forward.

**Addressing Config File Handling**

I’m checking how the function works with the configuration files in different directories. If the symlink isn't present, it creates a new directory in the home directory instead. This can lead to runtime data being saved outside of the dotfiles, which could be problematic. While the user wants to test it, and the config points to `/.config/matugen/colors.json`, this situation could conflict with future configurations. I need to proceed carefully to avoid issues with the stow process.

**Considering Stow Options**

I'm thinking about whether I can stow just the `.config/matugen` directory. If I use GNU Stow, I should consider it from the root of the package. This means I might need to ensure the entire repository is treated as one stow package. It's a bit of a puzzle, but I want to find a solution that keeps everything organized and functioning with minimal conflicts. I have to ensure I'm approaching this correctly!

I’ll install `matugen` via Cargo, then run a controlled color-source test through the Fish helper. Before generating output, I’ll avoid creating a non-stowed `~/.config/matugen` directory if it would interfere with the dotfiles symlink.

**Starting with examples**

I see that I shouldn't start with interjections or meta-commentary; I need to jump right in. I'll focus on being direct and including relevant examples. One essential question to address is, "How do I try mutagen?" I'll make sure to provide a clear answer for that. It’s `matugen`, not `mutagen`.

Matugen is mainly for generating color palettes from an image or color. It can change wallpapers, but our setup currently does **not** use it for wallpaper changes. We’re using it only to generate `~/.config/matugen/colors.json` for `matugen.nvim`.

Try it with a color:

```fish
matugen-generate --color '#7aa2f7'
```

Try it with an image/wallpaper file:

```fish
matugen-generate ~/Pictures/wallpaper.jpg
```

Then in Neovim:

```vim
:colorscheme matugen
```

Switch back anytime:

```vim
:colorscheme tokyonight
```

Useful variants:

```fish
matugen-generate --mode light --color '#7aa2f7'
matugen-generate --scheme scheme-expressive ~/Pictures/wallpaper.jpg
matugen-generate --scheme scheme-vibrant --color '#ff8800'
```

So the workflow is:

1. Pick a wallpaper/image or color.
2. Run `matugen-generate ...`.
3. Open Neovim and run `:colorscheme matugen`.

If you want, we can later wire a function that both sets the macOS wallpaper and regenerates the Neovim palette in one command.
I dont understand, what exactly does matugen do?

**Crafting a clear explanation**

I need to explain the user’s question directly and clearly, especially regarding "Material You." There might be a typo since they wrote "matugen." I should detail the exact pipeline of our setup and clarify that it doesn’t change all apps automatically unless modified. It's also important to mention that "Matugen is a color-palette generator and template renderer," covering input, processing, output, and use cases. I’ll also distinguish between the matugen CLI and the matugen.nvim plugin.

**Clarifying image processing**

I’m considering whether the image determines the dominant or appropriate source color. From that, it generates a Material You color palette for the app configuration, which in our setup only uses JSON. It’s essential to mention that changing the wallpaper requires a separate command or configuration. I might ask if the user wants to test with actual wallpaper or a mini function for future use. I should clarify that this is not “mutagen” but stick to a straightforward response.

Matugen does **theme generation**, not wallpaper management by default.

It takes an input:

```fish
matugen-generate --color '#7aa2f7'
```

or:

```fish
matugen-generate ~/Pictures/wallpaper.jpg
```

Then it generates a color palette from that input, like:

```json
{
  "primary": "#aec6ff",
  "secondary": "#bfc6dc",
  "surface": "#121318",
  "on_surface": "#e2e2e9"
}
```

In our setup, that gets written to:

```text
~/.config/matugen/colors.json
```

Then `matugen.nvim` reads that file and turns Neovim into a theme based on those colors.

So the flow is:

```text
wallpaper/image/color -> matugen -> colors.json -> matugen.nvim -> Neovim theme
```

It does **not** automatically change your wallpaper in our setup.

Matugen can be configured to run wallpaper commands too, but I deliberately did not wire that in yet. Right now it only generates colors for Neovim.

Practical example:

```fish
matugen-generate ~/Pictures/my-wallpaper.jpg
```

Then in Neovim:

```vim
:colorscheme matugen
```

That makes Neovim visually match the colors extracted from `my-wallpaper.jpg`.

So: matugen is useful if you want themes that follow a wallpaper or chosen color. It is not itself the wallpaper picker unless we configure it that way.
[Image 1] This is what I see... is this expected? The wallpaper colour seems different

**Clarifying wallpaper extraction**

I should consider that the user provided a screenshot and doesn't need any tool instructions. Maybe I can suggest a command to get the current macOS wallpaper path without actually running it since that could be a simple response. Also, I can mention that our generated palette from the color `#7aa2f7` might not match the actual wallpaper if the user didn't run `matugen-generate` on the image file itself. The color differences might stem from a few factors I'll highlight later.

**Explaining Neovim transparency**

I need to clarify that Neovim's configuration allows for transparency, meaning the terminal or window blends with the wallpaper beneath it. So, the background you see is the actual wallpaper coming through, while the text and UI colors are derived from the matugen palette. The tmux and statusline might continue to use Tokyo Night or powerkit colors, rather than matching the matugen setup. Also, lualine's theme is still set to auto and may not fully align with matugen's colors.

Yes, that looks expected for the current setup.

Why it doesn’t match perfectly:

1. We generated the test palette from `#7aa2f7`, not from your actual wallpaper.
2. `matugen` does not automatically read the current wallpaper unless we give it the wallpaper image path.
3. `matugen.nvim` changes Neovim colors, not tmux, terminal background, Ghostty/WezTerm opacity, or macOS wallpaper.
4. Your Neovim config is transparent, so you are seeing the wallpaper/terminal blur behind Neovim. That background is separate from the colors `matugen.nvim` applies to text/UI highlights.
5. Material You palettes are not exact image-color copies. They generate harmonized, contrast-safe colors from the source.

To test it properly, run it against the actual wallpaper file:

```fish
matugen-generate ~/Pictures/your-wallpaper.jpg
```

Then in Neovim:

```vim
:colorscheme matugen
```

What should change:
- Syntax colors
- File tree colors
- Statusline-ish UI highlights depending on plugin support
- Neovim foreground/surface colors

What will not change yet:
- macOS wallpaper
- tmux green border/statusline
- terminal theme/background
- Ghostty/WezTerm transparency or blur

So the current result is a partial Neovim theme experiment, not a whole-desktop dynamic theme yet.

To test it properly, run it against the actual wallpaper file:
matugen-generate ~/Pictures/your-wallpaper.jpg

So matugen isn't automated, i would need to update the matugen...

Correct. Right now it is **not automated**.

Current setup is:

```text
you run matugen-generate -> it updates colors.json -> Neovim can use :colorscheme matugen
```

So if you change wallpaper, you would need to rerun:

```fish
matugen-generate /path/to/new-wallpaper.jpg
```

What we have now is the safe/manual version.

Automation options:

1. **Manual but simple**
   - Keep `matugen-generate`.
   - You rerun it whenever you want the theme updated.
   - Lowest risk.

2. **One command for wallpaper + theme**
   - Add something like `wallpaper-set ~/Pictures/foo.jpg`.
   - It would set the macOS wallpaper and run `matugen-generate` from the same image.
   - Best practical next step.

3. **Auto-update Neovim when colors change**
   - Enable `matugen.nvim` watcher.
   - Then once `colors.json` changes, Neovim reloads automatically.
   - Still needs something to regenerate `colors.json`.

4. **Fully automatic wallpaper detection**
   - Watch for macOS wallpaper changes and regenerate colors automatically.
   - More fragile on macOS and probably overkill initially.

My recommendation: make option 2. A Fish function like:

```fish
wallpaper-set ~/Pictures/wallpaper.jpg
```

It would:

```text
set macOS wallpaper -> run matugen from that image -> write colors.json -> tell Neovim to use/reload matugen
```

That makes matugen feel useful without needing a background watcher.

3. Auto-update Neovim when colors change
   - Enable matugen.nvim watcher.
   - Then once colors.json changes, Neovim reloads automatically.
   - Still needs something to regenerate colors.json. 

How does matugen.nvim suggest doing it?
I’ll check the plugin’s README directly so the recommendation matches what `matugen.nvim` documents, not just our current wrapper.

state.output:
# matugen.nvim

A Neovim colorscheme that follows your wallpaper. Reads the JSON output from [matugen](https://github.com/InioX/matugen), maps it into a full editor palette in OKLCH, and reloads itself when the wallpaper changes.

It is opinionated: one vivid accent, calm everything else, diagnostics that stay red/yellow/green no matter what hue your wallpaper happens to be that day.

## Requirements

- Neovim 0.10+
- [matugen](https://github.com/InioX/matugen), configured to write a JSON file containing the Material You tokens listed below

## Install

With `lazy.nvim`:

```lua
{
  "daedlock/matugen.nvim",
  lazy = false,
  priority = 1000,
  config = function()
    require("matugen").setup({
      colors_path = "~/.config/matugen/colors.json",
    })
    vim.cmd.colorscheme("matugen")
  end,
}
```

## The JSON

The plugin reads a JSON file that you point it at via `colors_path`. It expects these keys (hex strings, with or without `#`):

```
surface_container_lowest   surface_dim                surface
surface_container_low      surface_container          surface_container_high
surface_container_highest  outline                    outline_variant
on_surface                 on_surface_variant         primary
secondary                  tertiary                   error
```

These are standard Material 3 token names — see [matugen's templating docs](https://github.com/InioX/matugen/blob/main/docs/configuration.md) for how to write a template that emits them. Keys you omit are filled in from a baseline palette, so a partial JSON still works (you just get less wallpaper-tinting on those slots).

If the file is missing entirely, the colorscheme still loads from the baseline so you can verify the install before configuring matugen.

## Auto-reload

`setup()` starts an `fs_event` watcher on `colors_path`. When matugen rewrites the file, the colorscheme reloads in place — no `:colorscheme` again, no restart. The watcher debounces by 150 ms so you don't get five reloads from a single rename-into-place write.

Toggle with `:MatugenWatch off` / `:MatugenWatch on`, or disable entirely:

```lua
require("matugen").setup({ watch = false })
```

After every reload the plugin fires a `User MatugenReloaded` autocmd, in case other parts of your config want to react.

## Configuration

Defaults:

```lua
require("matugen").setup({
  colors_path = "~/.config/matugen/colors.json",
  watch = true,

  -- OKLCH lightness offset on backgrounds. Positive = lighter editor bg.
  brightness = 0.03,

  -- OKLCH lightness offset on accents/foreground (independent of brightness).
  contrast = 0,

  -- Offset for sidebar panels (neo-tree, snacks explorer, mini.files) relative
  -- to the editor bg. Positive = brighter than editor, negative = darker.
  sidebar_brightness = 0.02,

  -- How aggressively to pull named accent hues toward the wallpaper's primary.
  -- 0 = canonical hues, ~30 = strongly tinted.
  harmonize = 15,

  -- Minimum L* contrast between accents and bg. Bumped up if matugen hands us
  -- a primary that's too close to the surface lightness.
  min_contrast = 0.45,

  -- Override any palette key after derivation. Same names as in palette.lua.
  overrides = {
    -- str = "#c8d3a0",
  },
})
```

The `:MatugenBrightness <n>` and `:MatugenContrast <n>` commands let you nudge the values live. `:MatugenReload` re-reads the JSON without changing config.

## Lualine

```lua
require("lualine").setup({
  options = { theme = require("matugen").lualine() },
})
```

Re-call this after `User MatugenReloaded` if you want lualine to track wallpaper changes:

```lua
vim.api.nvim_create_autocmd("User", {
  pattern = "MatugenReloaded",
  callback = function()
    require("lualine").setup({
      options = { theme = require("matugen").lualine() },
    })
  end,
})
```

## Design notes

The palette is built around three chroma tiers:

- **Hero** — the wallpaper's primary hue at full chroma. Owns keywords, the cursor, the active accent. One color, one job.
- **Cast** — supporting identifiers (functions, types, constants) at low chroma. Distinct hues, but quiet. They're there to be parsed, not noticed.
- **Whisper** — data (strings, numbers, properties) at near-grey chroma. Recedes so structure stands out.

Diagnostics break the rule: errors, warnings, and successes use high chroma at canonical red/yellow/green hues, only lightly harmonized toward the wallpaper. They have to be unmistakable.

Background and surface tokens come straight from matugen's surface ramp — no recomputation. Matugen already knows how to tint surfaces relative to a wallpaper; the plugin just borrows that work.

## Building on the palette

```lua
local p = require("matugen").get_palette()
-- p.bg, p.fg, p.accent, p.kw, p.fn, p.type, p.str, p.error, ...
```

The palette is also re-derived on every reload, so capturing `p` once and stashing it will go stale. Read it inside callbacks if you care about live updates.

For your own color math:

```lua
local color = require("matugen").color()
local lch = color.hex_to_oklch("#3a8fb7")
local hex = color.oklch(lch.L, lch.C * 0.5, lch.h)
```

## Supported plugins

Highlight groups are defined for: Treesitter, LSP semantic tokens, diagnostics (including `tiny-inline-diagnostic`), gitsigns, snacks (picker, explorer, notifier, terminal, dashboard), telescope (fallback), neo-tree, mini.files, oil, trouble, which-key, blink/cmp, lazy, notify, barbecue, lualine, and the standard editor UI groups.

If something looks wrong in a plugin not on that list, open an issue with a screenshot.

## License

MIT

metadata.preview:
# matugen.nvim

A Neovim colorscheme that follows your wallpaper. Reads the JSON output from [matugen](https://github.com/InioX/matugen), maps it into a full editor palette in OKLCH, and reloads itself when the wallpaper changes.

It is opinionated: one vivid accent, calm everything else, diagnostics that stay red/yellow/green no matter what hue your wallpaper happens to be that day.

## Requirements

- Neovim 0.10+
- [matugen](https://github.com/InioX/matugen), configured to write a JSON file containing the Material You tokens listed below

## Install

With `lazy.nvim`:

```lua
{
  "daedlock/matugen.nvim",
  lazy = false,
  priority = 1000,

parts.text:
**Considering plan mode behavior**

I'm thinking that when plan mode is active, I shouldn't offer changes. Instead, I could ask if the user wants to switch to implementation. The developer mentioned that when plan mode is active, it's read-only, so the final answer can still include the plan. Also, it might be better not to start with "Got it." I should remember to keep everything clear and avoid nested bullets—headers are optional too.

`matugen.nvim` suggests a two-part setup:

1. `matugen` generates `~/.config/matugen/colors.json`.
2. `matugen.nvim` watches that JSON file and reloads Neovim colors when it changes.

From its README, the intended Neovim config is basically:

```lua
require("matugen").setup({
  colors_path = "~/.config/matugen/colors.json",
})
vim.cmd.colorscheme("matugen")
```

By default, `matugen.nvim` sets:

```lua
watch = true
```

So when `matugen` rewrites `colors.json`, Neovim reloads the theme automatically. It does **not** itself detect your wallpaper or run `matugen`; it only watches the generated JSON file.

Our current experiment is more conservative:

```lua
watch = false
```

So right now, after regenerating colors you may need:

```vim
:MatugenReload
```

or re-run:

```vim
:colorscheme matugen
```

What `matugen.nvim` expects is:

```text
wallpaper changes somehow
-> matugen runs and rewrites ~/.config/matugen/colors.json
-> matugen.nvim sees colors.json changed
-> Neovim theme reloads automatically
```

So the missing automation is not in `matugen.nvim`; it is the step that runs `matugen` whenever your wallpaper changes.

Best practical setup for us would be:

```fish
wallpaper-set ~/Pictures/foo.jpg
```

That function would:

```text
set macOS wallpaper
run matugen-generate ~/Pictures/foo.jpg
matugen.nvim watcher reloads Neovim automatically
```

And in Neovim we would change our config to match the plugin’s intended behavior by enabling `watch = true`.

Yes. That is exactly the missing piece.

`matugen.nvim` only watches `colors.json`. It does not know when macOS wallpaper changes.

So you need one of these workflows:

1. **Recommended: change wallpaper through our own command**
   - Add a Fish function like `wallpaper-set ~/Pictures/foo.jpg`.
   - It sets the macOS wallpaper.
   - It runs `matugen-generate ~/Pictures/foo.jpg`.
   - `matugen.nvim` watches `colors.json` and reloads automatically.

2. **Manual fallback**
   - Change wallpaper however you want.
   - Then run:

```fish
matugen-generate /path/to/current-wallpaper.jpg
```

3. **Fully automatic watcher**
   - A background process polls/detects current wallpaper changes.
   - When it changes, it runs `matugen-generate`.
   - This is more fragile on macOS and probably not worth starting with.

Best next implementation:

```text
wallpaper-set IMAGE
-> set macOS wallpaper to IMAGE
-> run matugen-generate IMAGE
-> colors.json changes
-> matugen.nvim auto-reloads
```

And update Neovim from:

```lua

```

to:

```lua

```

That gives you an automated flow as long as you use `wallpaper-set` to change wallpapers.

**Formulating a sync plan**

I need to provide a plan for the user on how to synchronize wallpaper across devices. I’ll start by inspecting existing wallpaper and sync patterns while keeping it in read-only mode. **Considering wallpaper storage**

I’m contemplating whether to store the selected wallpaper ID or name in dotfiles or iCloud. It’s essential to remember that we're in plan mode, so no edits to existing files can happen. For cross-device syncing, I could propose a Git-backed `wallpapers/` directory in dotfiles that tracks the selected file in `.config/matugen/wallpaper-current`. function matugen-generate --description "Generate matugen.nvim colors from an image or source color"
    set -l mode dark
    set -l scheme scheme-tonal-spot
    set -l source ""
    set -l force_color false

    set -l i 1
    while test $i -le (count $argv)
        set -l arg $argv[$i]

        switch $arg
            case -h --help help
                _matugen_generate_help
                return 0
            case -m --mode
                set i (math $i + 1)
                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
                    return 1
                end

I’m considering syncing wallpapers. One idea is to use Git to version my dotfiles, but that could lead to bloat due to binary files. I could track just a few or use Git LFS to manage large files, but maintaining a personal repo could be tricky. Another option might be using S3 with rclone, but that feels like overkill. I’m thinking of storing the current wallpaper in a text file within my dotfiles or syncing it through iCloud/Syncthing for simpler management across devices.

**Exploring wallpaper automation**

I’m considering whether to create a script that uses `fswatch` to trigger an action every N seconds. I should check if there’s a Brewfile for `fswatch`. Using LaunchAgent with `WatchPaths` could work for files in a synced folder, triggering `wallpaper-apply-current`. But I wonder if I can expand the environment variable in the path—might need a wrapper script since my configs are stowed in dotfiles, making it a bit more complex. 

For the Neovim watcher, it doesn’t need cross-device syncing since each machine regenerates its own `colors.json`. I should mention not syncing the generated `colors.json` and also avoid syncing macOS wallpaper settings directly. I need to clarify that the screenshot looks different due to terminal settings not synced by matugen.

**Including wallpaper commands**

I think if I’m setting the wallpaper command, I should provide sample commands to clarify the process. For example, I could use:

```
wallpaper-set /Pictures/Wallpapers/sunset.jpg
```

This not only updates the wallpaper but also updates the color palette. If Neovim has matugen loaded and the watcher is active, it will automatically reload with the new settings. This would ensure a smooth and cohesive experience with updating wallpapers and colors in my setup.

Yes. The missing automation is:

```text
wallpaper changes -> run matugen -> colors.json changes -> matugen.nvim reloads
```

**How I’d Build It**
Add a Fish function called `wallpaper-set`.

Usage:

```fish
wallpaper-set ~/Pictures/Wallpapers/foo.jpg
```

It would do three things:

```text
1. Set the macOS wallpaper to foo.jpg
2. Run matugen-generate foo.jpg
3. Let matugen.nvim reload from the updated colors.json
```

The core macOS command would be:

```fish
osascript -e "tell application \"System Events\" to tell every desktop to set picture to POSIX file \"$image\""
```

The function shape would be:

```fish
function wallpaper-set --description "Set wallpaper and regenerate matugen theme"
    set -l image (path resolve $argv[1])

    if not test -f "$image"
        echo "Wallpaper not found: $image" >&2
        return 1
    end

    osascript -e "tell application \"System Events\" to tell every desktop to set picture to POSIX file \"$image\""
    or return 1

    matugen-generate "$image"
end
```

Then Neovim should change from:

```lua
watch = false
```

to:

```lua
watch = true
```

Important detail: with our current Neovim config, `matugen.nvim` is optional/lazy. The watcher only matters once you are actually using `:colorscheme matugen`. If you want matugen to be the default always-follow-wallpaper theme, we would make it non-lazy and set it as the default colorscheme.

**Cross-Device Sync**
There are two separate sync problems:

```text
1. Sync the wallpaper image files
2. Sync which wallpaper is currently selected
```

I would **not** sync `colors.json`. It is generated output and should stay per-device.

Best options for wallpaper files:

1. **iCloud Drive**
   - Best if all devices are Macs.
   - Put wallpapers in something like:

```text
~/Library/Mobile Documents/com~apple~CloudDocs/Wallpapers/
```

   - Every Mac gets the same files.
   - `wallpaper-set` can use paths from that folder.

2. **Syncthing**
   - Better if you want Mac/Linux/cross-platform sync.
   - Sync `~/Pictures/Wallpapers/` across devices.
   - More setup, but more controllable than iCloud.

3. **Git/dotfiles**
   - Possible, but I do not recommend it unless it is a tiny curated wallpaper set.
   - Wallpapers are binary files and will bloat the dotfiles repo.

**Recommended Cross-Device Design**
Use a synced wallpaper folder plus a small “current wallpaper” file.

Example:

```text
~/Pictures/Wallpapers/
  aurora.jpg
  forest.jpg
  city.jpg
  .current-wallpaper
```

When you run:

```fish
wallpaper-set ~/Pictures/Wallpapers/aurora.jpg
```

it would:

```text
set macOS wallpaper
run matugen-generate
write aurora.jpg into .current-wallpaper
```

On another Mac, you could run:

```fish
wallpaper-apply-current
```

That would read `.current-wallpaper`, set the same wallpaper, and regenerate that device’s `colors.json`.

Later, if you want full automation, add a macOS LaunchAgent that watches `.current-wallpaper` and runs `wallpaper-apply-current` when it changes.

My recommendation: implement in this order:

1. Add `wallpaper-set`
2. Enable `matugen.nvim` `watch = true`
3. Use iCloud Drive or Syncthing for `Wallpapers/`
4. Add `wallpaper-apply-current`
5. Only then consider a LaunchAgent for automatic cross-device application

That gives you a clean manual workflow first, then cross-device sync without making the setup fragile.

**Considering AppleScript Solution**

It seems like a useful alternative to change the desktop picture. I want to make sure I’m clear on how to format that command properly so it works as intended. It’s interesting to explore different options for accomplishing the same goal!

**Executing code updates**

I'm noticing that the earlier attempt to get every desktop picture returned a missing value, but the settings might still work. I need to check if imageFile is an alias. I might have to update the `matugen-generate` function since it currently creates a directory in the config path. If that's a symlink, I'll manage that too. The user asked if I executed it, and I haven't, so I'll implement the execution and patch the files now.

I’m going to add a concrete `wallpaper-set` Fish function now and turn on the `matugen.nvim` watcher. The test will use an explicit macOS system wallpaper because the current dynamic wallpaper path is not exposed by `System Events` on this machine.

input.patchText:
*** Begin Patch
*** Add File: /Users/shahe.islam/dotfiles/.config/fish/functions/wallpaper-set.fish
+function wallpaper-set --description "Set macOS wallpaper and regenerate matugen colors"
+    set -l mode dark
+    set -l scheme scheme-tonal-spot
+    set -l image ""
+
+    set -l i 1
+    while test $i -le (count $argv)
+        set -l arg $argv[$i]
+
+        switch $arg
+            case -h --help help
+                _wallpaper_set_help
+                return 0
+            case -m --mode
+                set i (math $i + 1)
+                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
+                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set mode $argv[$i]
+            case --scheme
+                set i (math $i + 1)
+                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
+                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set scheme $argv[$i]
+            case '*'
+                if test -n "$image"
+                    echo "Unexpected extra argument: $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set image $arg
+        end
+
+        set i (math $i + 1)
+    end
+
+    if test -z "$image"
+        _wallpaper_set_help >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    set image (path resolve -- "$image")
+    if not test -f "$image"
+        echo "Wallpaper not found: $image" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    if not command -q osascript
+        echo "osascript is required to set the macOS wallpaper" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    osascript \
+        -e 'on run argv' \
+        -e 'set imageFile to POSIX file (item 1 of argv)' \
+        -e 'tell application "System Events"' \
+        -e 'repeat with desktopItem in desktops' \
+        -e 'set picture of desktopItem to imageFile' \
+        -e 'end repeat' \
+        -e 'end tell' \
+        -e 'end run' \
+        "$image"
+    or return 1
+
+    matugen-generate --mode "$mode" --scheme "$scheme" "$image"
+end
+
+function _wallpaper_set_help --description "Show wallpaper-set usage"
+    echo "wallpaper-set - set macOS wallpaper and regenerate matugen.nvim colors"

@@ -0,0 +1,81 @@
+function wallpaper-set --description "Set macOS wallpaper and regenerate matugen colors"
+    set -l mode dark
+    set -l scheme scheme-tonal-spot
+    set -l image ""
+
+    set -l i 1
+    while test $i -le (count $argv)
+        set -l arg $argv[$i]
+
+        switch $arg
+            case -h --help help
+                _wallpaper_set_help
+                return 0
+            case -m --mode
+                set i (math $i + 1)
+                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
+                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set mode $argv[$i]
+            case --scheme
+                set i (math $i + 1)
+                if test $i -gt (count $argv)
+                    echo "Missing value for $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set scheme $argv[$i]
+            case '*'
+                if test -n "$image"
+                    echo "Unexpected extra argument: $arg" >&2
+                    return 1
+                end
+                set image $arg
+        end
+
+        set i (math $i + 1)
+    end
+
+    if test -z "$image"
+        _wallpaper_set_help >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    set image (path resolve -- "$image")
+    if not test -f "$image"
+        echo "Wallpaper not found: $image" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    if not command -q osascript
+        echo "osascript is required to set the macOS wallpaper" >&2
+        return 1
+    end
+
+    osascript \
+        -e 'on run argv' \
+        -e 'set imageFile to POSIX file (item 1 of argv)' \
+        -e 'tell application "System Events"' \
+        -e 'repeat with desktopItem in desktops' \
+        -e 'set picture of desktopItem to imageFile' \
+        -e 'end repeat' \
+        -e 'end tell' \
+        -e 'end run' \
+        "$image"
+    or return 1
+
+    matugen-generate --mode "$mode" --scheme "$scheme" "$image"
+end
+
+function _wallpaper_set_help --description "Show wallpaper-set usage"
+    echo "wallpaper-set - set macOS wallpaper and regenerate matugen.nvim colors"
+    echo ""
+    echo "Usage:"
+    echo "  wallpaper-set <image-path>"
+    echo "  wallpaper-set --mode light <image-path>"
+    echo "  wallpaper-set --scheme scheme-expressive <image-path>"
+    echo ""
+    echo "Output:"
+    echo "  Sets every macOS desktop wallpaper"
+    echo "  Regenerates ~/.config/matugen/colors.json"
+end

parts.text:
**Fixing wallpaper automation**

I realized there might be an issue with the wallpaper being set before matugen crashes. It seems like matugen changed the wallpaper to Sonoma. I need to handle that properly. Matugen v4.1 claims to support JXL, but HEIC might be unsupported, leading to complications. I could also modify the function to run matugen first before setting the wallpaper; this way, if matugen fails, the wallpaper won't change. Perhaps generating the image first and then setting the wallpaper could avoid the color issues altogether. Plus, I need to consider HEIC support too!

**Improving wallpaper setup**

I’m thinking that I need to modify the process to run `matugen-generate` before using `osascript` to avoid setting an unsupported image. If matugen supports JPG and PNG, that's good — I should test with a PNG. I also want to update the help documentation to suggest JPG/PNG and note that HEIC may not work. We should check if matugen runs first; if it fails, the wallpaper won’t change. I might need to ask the user if they want to restore their previous wallpaper. Testing with a solid color PNG like "Electric Blue.png" could help with automation.

**Deciding wallpaper process**

I’m considering the implications of the wallpaper setup. If setting the wallpaper fails, it could change the theme, which isn't ideal. The command needs to handle setting the wallpaper and generating the image efficiently. For reliability, I think I should run `matugen` first to ensure it can read the image. If matugen succeeds, then I can set the wallpaper. If the wallpaper fails, at least the colors will be updated to reflect the image. I don’t think I should set the wallpaper first, though.

The first real automation test exposed an important edge case: macOS can set `.heic` wallpapers, but `matugen 4.1.0` failed to read that `.heic` and crashed. I’m patching the function so color generation happens before changing the wallpaper, which prevents unsupported images from changing the desktop without a matching palette.

parts.text:
**Clarifying Neovim behavior**

I’ve noted that the current wallpaper is Space Gray, which is good! Now, I need to mention that the user won’t see changes in their Neovim screenshot immediately because the plugin needs to reload if it was loaded with watch=false. Switching to watch=true will apply to new sessions only. If they're in their current session, they can run `:MatugenWatch on` after setting `:colorscheme matugen` to see the updates live.

Yes, I executed it now.

What I changed:
- Added `wallpaper-set`
- Enabled `matugen.nvim` watcher with `watch = true`
- Tested the full flow

Command executed:

```fish
wallpaper-set "/System/Library/Desktop Pictures/Solid Colors/Space Gray.png"
```

Result:
- macOS wallpaper was set to `Space Gray.png`
- `~/.config/matugen/colors.json` was regenerated
- `~/.config/matugen/current-wallpaper` was written
- `nvim --headless "+colorscheme matugen" +qa` passed
- `scripts/test-filter.sh fish` passed: 52/52

Important discovery:
- Apple `.heic` wallpapers did **not** work with `matugen`; it crashed trying to read `Sonoma.heic`.
- I patched `wallpaper-set` so it now runs `matugen` **before** changing the wallpaper. That prevents unsupported images from changing your wallpaper without a matching theme.
- Use `.png`/`.jpg` wallpapers for now unless we add an HEIC conversion step.

For your already-open Neovim session, run:

```vim
:colorscheme matugen
:MatugenWatch on
```

For new Neovim sessions, `watch = true` is now configured automatically once `matugen` is loaded. No commits or pushes made.
i've ran these commands, now how do i test the workflow

**Providing workflow testing steps**

The user is asking how to test the workflow after running certain commands like `:colorscheme matugen` and `:MatugenWatch on`. I need to suggest a concise sequence for them. They should open Neovim with the matugen colorscheme and run the wallpaper-set command with two PNG/JPG wallpapers to see the color changes. I’ll advise using two solid PNGs for clarity and mention that HEIC isn't supported. Also, I should suggest checking the colors.json before and after, or using checksum for verification.

**Outlining testing steps in Neovim**

To test the workflow in Neovim, first, I need to ensure the colorscheme is set properly with `:colorscheme matugen` and have `:MatugenWatch on`. Then, I should run the command `wallpaper-set "/System/Library/Desktop Pictures/Solid Colors/Electric Blue.png"`, followed by `wallpaper-set "/System/Library/Desktop Pictures/Solid Colors/Red Orange.png"` to check for changes. If they aren’t visually obvious, I suggest using commands to inspect the colors in the colors.json file. If there’s still no change, I should verify that the colorscheme is set correctly and it’s not using HEIC wallpapers.

Test it by switching between two obvious PNG wallpapers while Neovim is open.

In Neovim, make sure matugen is active:

```vim
:colorscheme matugen
:MatugenWatch on
```

Then in a Fish shell, run:

```fish
wallpaper-set "/System/Library/Desktop Pictures/Solid Colors/Electric Blue.png"
```

Watch for:
- macOS wallpaper changes
- Neovim colors update automatically within a second
- `~/.config/matugen/colors.json` changes

Then test a second obvious color:

```fish
wallpaper-set "/System/Library/Desktop Pictures/Solid Colors/Red Orange.png"
```

Verify from shell:

```fish
cat ~/.config/matugen/current-wallpaper
jq '.primary, .secondary, .surface' ~/.config/matugen/colors.json
```

Verify from Neovim:

```vim
:echo g:colors_name
```

Expected output:

```vim
matugen
```

If Neovim does not update automatically, run:

```vim
:MatugenReload
```

Important: test with `.png` or `.jpg` files for now. Apple `.heic` wallpapers currently fail in `matugen`.

When i run ~/Pictures➜  wallpaper-set ~/Downloads/tobias-reich-JE56S222Rw0-unsplash.jpg

it goes from [Image 1] to [Image 2]

**Diagnosing wallpaper issue**

The user ran a command that changed their wallpaper from a transparent tint to an opaque dark background in Neovim. It looks like matugen.nvim might be overriding the expected transparency settings. I need to diagnose the issue and implement a fix to maintain a transparent background when using this theme. I'll also inspect the relevant transparency settings, as it seems they could be affecting how matugen handles the color scheme. The user likely expects the terminal wallpaper to remain visible.

**Inspecting Neovim configurations**

I'm considering adding overrides or autocmds to clear backgrounds after reloading the matugen theme and colorscheme. I need to examine the current Neovim configuration, especially regarding transparency and styling. It seems that the matugen theme sets opaque backgrounds, which is causing the visible wallpaper to disappear. I'll also check if the lualine theme is correctly refreshed. The primary issue is the loss of the transparent background that needs fixing. I'll prepare for a step-by-step update for the developer while inspecting the Neovim files.

That transition shows the automation is working, but `matugen.nvim` is making Neovim’s backgrounds opaque. I’m going to patch the Neovim styling layer so matugen reloads preserve your existing transparent-terminal workflow.

1: -- Styling Autocmds - Consistent visual styling across all colorschemes
2: -- This module ensures italics, bold, and other styling is consistent regardless of theme
3: 
4: local M = {}
5: 
6: local function augroup(name)
7:   return vim.api.nvim_create_augroup("styling_" .. name, { clear = true })
8: end
9: 
10: -- Helper function to merge styles with existing colors
11: local function merge_style(group, new_style)
12:   -- Get existing highlight group (includes colors from theme)
13:   local existing = vim.api.nvim_get_hl(0, { name = group, link = false })
14: 
15:   -- Merge new styles (italic/bold) with existing colors (fg/bg/sp)
16:   -- This preserves theme colors while applying consistent styling
17:   local merged = vim.tbl_extend("force", existing, new_style)
18: 
19:   -- Apply the merged highlight
20:   vim.api.nvim_set_hl(0, group, merged)
21: end
22:

25:   -- Tree-sitter highlight groups (modern, preferred method)
26:   local ts_highlights = {
27:     -- Comments

-- Styling Autocmds - Consistent visual styling across all colorschemes
-- This module ensures italics, bold, and other styling is consistent regardless of theme

local M = {}

local function augroup(name)
  return vim.api.nvim_create_augroup("styling_" .. name, { clear = true })
end

-- Helper function to merge styles with existing colors
local function merge_style(group, new_style)
  -- Get existing highlight group (includes colors from theme)
  local existing = vim.api.nvim_get_hl(0, { name = group, link = false })

  -- Merge new styles (italic/bold) with existing colors (fg/bg/sp)
  -- This preserves theme colors while applying consistent styling
  local merged = vim.tbl_extend("force", existing, new_style)

  -- Apply the merged highlight
  vim.api.nvim_set_hl(0, group, merged)
